title: Catechist Guide — Confirmation
html:
Catechist Guide

To prepare and lead the lesson. For the catechist's exclusive use.

Lesson Summary

Estimated duration: 60–75 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: Confirmation as personal Pentecost: the gift of the Holy Spirit that makes us courageous witnesses of Christ and equips us for mission.

Objectives:

  • For the student to understand Confirmation not as a “graduation” but as a “commissioning” and an “equipping.”
  • To know the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit and understand how they manifest in daily life.
  • To discover, through the witness of young saints like José Sánchez del Río and Joan of Arc, that Confirmation gives real strength for bearing witness to the faith.
  • To distinguish between the matter, form, minister, and effects of the sacrament.
  • To assimilate that Confirmation imprints an indelible character: it is not a passing rite but a permanent transformation.

Key verse: Acts 1:8 (“You shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you; and you shall be my witnesses”)

Catechism references: CCC 1285–1321

Suggested Lesson Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Welcome and icebreaker 5 min Greet, create a welcoming atmosphere. Ask: “If you could have one superpower, what would you choose?” Listen. Connect: “The Holy Spirit doesn't give superpowers. He gives something far better: gifts that transform the soul.”
2. The story of José Sánchez del Río 10 min Tell José's martyrdom with passion. If possible, show a clip from For Greater Glory. Ask: “Would you have done what he did? Where did a 14-year-old get that strength?” Guide the answer toward the Holy Spirit received at Confirmation.
3. Main content 25 min Present the 7 content sections, adapting the pace to the group's age. Emphasize particularly: the 7 gifts (each with a concrete, contemporary example), the biblical foundation in Acts 8 and 19, the 5 effects, and the fact that Confirmation does not “expire.”
4. Group activity 15 min Choose an activity according to age: children → draw the 7-gifts shield; teens → write the letter to a lapsed friend; adults → discuss in pairs the ideal age for Confirmation. See suggestions below.
5. Sharing 8 min Volunteers share their shield, their letter, or their conclusions. Never force. Value every contribution. Ask: “Did anyone discover something today you didn't know before?”
6. Closing prayer 7 min Pray the lesson's prayer together. If possible, sing Veni Sancte Spiritus (Taizé) or Veni Creator Spiritus. Finish with each person silently invoking the gift they most need.

Notes for the Catechist

On St. José Sánchez del Río

José Sánchez del Río was born on March 28, 1913, in Sahuayo, Michoacán, Mexico. He received Confirmation and First Communion as a child. When the Cristero War broke out (1926), his older brothers joined the Cristero army. José, at just 13, begged his mother to let him enlist. His mother's reply (“You're too young”) and the boy's retort (“You're never too young to give your life for Christ”) are historic. He was admitted as a flag-bearer.

On February 5, 1928, he was captured in combat near Cotija. His captors imprisoned him in the sacristy of the local church (turned into barracks) and offered him freedom if he betrayed the Cristeros or renounced his faith. José refused. On February 10, they brought him out, sliced the soles of his feet with a machete, and forced him to walk to the town cemetery. Witnesses reported that José sang the Cristero hymn: “Holy Virgin, my Mother, give me your blessing. I go to fight for your cause, I go to defend your honor.” At the cemetery, before the coup de grâce, the commander asked if he wanted to send a message to his family. José replied: “That we'll see each other in Heaven. ¡Viva Cristo Rey! ¡Viva la Virgen de Guadalupe!” He was beatified by Benedict XVI in 2005 and canonized by Francis in 2016.

Visual resource: Show an image of St. José Sánchez del Río. The real photos from his wake (bandaged feet) are easily found online. There is also modern iconography. The film Cristiada (For Greater Glory) contains the martyrdom scene (starting approximately at minute 110). It is intense but appropriate for teenagers.

Pedagogical insight: The Mexican connection is exceptionally powerful for Hispanic/Latino groups. If your students are of Mexican heritage, ask: “Has anyone in your family heard stories about the Cristeros?” For non-Latino groups, the story is equally stunning because of José's age and the sheer courage displayed. Frame it as a universal example of what the Holy Spirit can do in a young person.

On St. Joan of Arc

Joan was born around 1412 in Domrémy, France, to a peasant family. At age 13, she began to hear voices — St. Michael, St. Catherine, and St. Margaret — asking her to liberate France from English occupation during the Hundred Years' War. At 17, she convinced the Dauphin Charles and led the French army to lift the siege of Orleans (1429). She wore armor, carried a banner bearing the names of Jesus and Mary, and never killed anyone in battle. Captured by the Burgundians, sold to the English, and tried by a hostile ecclesiastical court, she was condemned for heresy and for wearing men's clothing (in reality, she did so to protect herself in prison). She was burned at the stake in Rouen on May 30, 1431, at age 19. Her last word was “Jesus!” She was rehabilitated in 1456 and canonized by Benedict XV in 1920. She is the patroness of France.

Joan was never sacramentally confirmed (the adolescent Confirmation practice didn't exist in her era), but her life is the perfect model of what Confirmation produces: a young person anointed by the Holy Spirit for a mission that surpasses every human capacity. If you use her example, make this clarification and connect it: “She lived the fruits of Confirmation without having received it yet, just as we can live those fruits before receiving the sacrament, by the grace of God.”

On the Seven Gifts

This is arguably the doctrinal heart of the lesson. Devote at least 10–12 minutes to it. Give a concrete, contemporary example of each gift (the lesson provides them). If your group is teenagers, ask them to provide examples from their own lives.

Many people confuse the gifts of the Holy Spirit with the fruits (Gal 5:22-23). Clarify this briefly: the gifts are permanent capacities given for personal sanctity and mission; the fruits are the visible results of living according to the Spirit.

Key pastoral note: Many adults have never meditated on the seven gifts. You may find that this section sparks more conversation and breakthroughs than any other part of the lesson. Be ready to pause and go deeper.

On the Danger of “Graduation Mentality”

The phrase “Confirmation is graduation from the Church” is perhaps the most widespread pastoral heresy among young Latin American Catholics — and the English-speaking world has its own version: “I got confirmed, I've done my part.” With humor but firmness, dismantle this from the very first minute. Ask: “When a soldier completes basic training, do they go home to watch Netflix, or do they head to the front lines?” Confirmation is the COMMISSIONING, not the farewell.

If a young person says “I was confirmed and I stopped going,” do not judge them. Say: “The grace is there, asleep. You just have to wake it up. The Holy Spirit never left.”

Age Adaptation

🌱 Children (7–10 years)

Focus: Be concrete, visual, and don't overload them doctrinally. Center on: the Holy Spirit as wind and fire (Pentecost, Acts 2) + the chrism smells nice because it's perfumed oil + the gifts as “presents from heaven” + Joselito's story told as a tale of courage, without graphic details of the martyrdom (say simply that “they killed him because he loved Jesus”). The ideal activity: drawing the 7-gifts shield. Bring paper with a shield pre-drawn. Ask at the end: “When the bishop puts the oil on your forehead, what will you ask the Holy Spirit for?”

🌿 Pre-teens (11–13 years)

Focus: They are at immediate preparation age. Connect with their daily life: “You're growing up, making your own decisions, sometimes your friends pressure you… who helps you be brave?” The story of José Sánchez works perfectly: a boy close to their own age. Explain the gifts with examples from their world: messaging apps, school, friends, gaming. The gift of counsel: “When you don't know whether to share a secret or not, the Holy Spirit illuminates you.” The gift of fortitude: “When everyone in the group chat mocks the kid who goes to Mass, you don't laugh.” Activity: letter to a lapsed friend (adapt: letter to a friend who doesn't understand why you go to catechesis).

🍀 Teens (14–17 years)

Focus: Existential and combative. The language of “spiritual battle” and miles Christi connects with their search for an epic identity. If possible, show clips from For Greater Glory. Open the debate about the age of Confirmation and why it's conferred at this age: “Because this is when you're deciding who you're going to be.” Connect the gifts directly to the real challenges of their age: chastity, social pressure, digital addiction, crisis of faith. The gift of knowledge is key here: “Knowing that screens don't fill the void is wisdom from the Spirit.” Music: play Hakuna as an example that faith can be sung in their own language. Weekly challenge: let them choose one.

🌲 Adults (18+)

Focus: Recovering and going deeper. Many adults received Confirmation with poor preparation and need to reconcile with this sacrament. Opening question: “Does anyone remember anything from their Confirmation preparation?” Deeper theology: indelible character, Baptism/Confirmation distinction, connection with the other sacraments. The age debate challenges them as parents and potential catechists. The gifts of the Spirit may be the most illuminating section: many have never truly meditated on them. Activity: in pairs, each person tells the other which gift they most need right now and why. End with the Veni Creator Spiritus invocation.

Difficult Questions (And How to Respond)

“I was confirmed and I didn't feel anything. It was just like before.”

Sacramental grace doesn't depend on what we feel but on what it is. You may not have felt anything, but something objective happened: you were sealed, you received the gifts in their fullness, Confirmation imprinted a character on your soul. It's like a seed: when you plant it, you don't see anything right away. The tree takes time. But the seed is there. The question isn't “what did you feel” but “what have you done to help that seed grow”: prayer, sacraments, formation, works of mercy.

“If you can't feel it, how do I know it did anything?”

By the fruits. Not always immediate or spectacular. But if you look back and see that, despite your falls, you've kept believing, that there's something in you that resists settling for a mediocre life… that's the Holy Spirit at work. St. Paul says: “The Spirit helps us in our weakness” (Rom 8:26). He doesn't promise absence of struggle; he promises strength within the struggle.

“Why do some confirmed people live worse than those who weren't confirmed?”

Because grace isn't magic. It respects freedom. God, who created us free, does not force us even after pouring out his Spirit. The example of Judas is the strongest: he was chosen as an apostle, received the same gifts as the other eleven… and betrayed Christ. Confirmation doesn't “shield” you from sin; it equips you for battle. But you still have to wear the armor yourself, every day.

“St. José Sánchez del Río was a fanatic, not a hero.”

Don't respond defensively. Ask: “What is a fanatic?” Typically, the difference between a martyr and a fanatic is that the fanatic kills for their cause; the martyr dies for it. José never wielded a weapon; he was the flag-bearer. He died forgiving (“We'll see each other in heaven”). His death was an act of love, not hate. That is the opposite of fanaticism. Fanaticism fears and hates; true faith loves and trusts.

“Why can't Confirmation be repeated if someone received it poorly prepared?”

Confirmation, like Baptism, imprints a character: an indelible, permanent mark that configures the soul to Christ forever (CCC 1304-1305). God doesn't “erase” his gifts because we received them poorly. The solution is not to repeat the sacrament (which is impossible), but to renew the disposition. You can renew your baptismal promises, make a good confession, and ask for a fresh outpouring of the Holy Spirit through a retreat or spiritual renewal. Confirmation is there, in your soul, waiting. You only need to “activate” it.

“In my parish, Confirmation is at age 15, but in another it's at age 7. Which one is correct?”

Both are valid. The Catechism does not fix a universal age (CCC 1307). The Latin Church allows each episcopal conference to decide. What matters is not the age but the preparation and the disposition. The ideal theologically would be the ancient order (Baptism–Confirmation–Communion), but pastoral practice varies according to circumstances. Respect both. Yours is the one your bishop has decided for your diocese.

Supplementary Resources for the Catechist

  • YouCat, questions 200–206 — The Youth Catechism on Confirmation. Ideal for teens. Available free at youcat.org and in the app.
  • CCC Compendium, 296–305 — The shorter Compendium summarizes Confirmation in 10 Q&A's. Excellent to review before teaching the full lesson.
  • Paul VI, Apostolic Constitution Divinae consortium naturae (1971) — The document that reformed the rite of Confirmation. Defines the effects with the formula quoted by the Catechism.
  • Ascension Presents (Fr. Mike Schmitz) — Search for “Confirmation explained” on YouTube. Clear and engaging. Also, the Catechism in a Year podcast, days covering CCC 1285–1321.
  • Opus Dei, Topic 37: “The Sacrament of Confirmation” — In the series “Summaries of Catholic Teaching” at opusdei.org. Brief, clear, profound. Free.
  • Film For Greater Glory (2012) — If you can show even 5 minutes, the martyrdom scene of San José Sánchez del Río is stunning and transformative.
  • Music: Hakuna Group Music (various songs), Taizé (Veni Sancte Spiritus), Veni Creator Spiritus (Gregorian chant).
  • Bishop Robert Barron / Word on Fire — Short videos on the Holy Spirit and the sacraments. Available at wordonfire.org and YouTube.
  • Real + True (OSV) — Multimedia Catechism at osvnews.com/real-true. Episode on Confirmation covers CCC 1285–1321 with accessible modern language.
  • St. John Paul II, Rise, Let Us Be on Our Way — The anecdote about his Confirmation day is at the beginning of the book. A short but powerful excerpt to read aloud.

Catechist's Prayer

Come, Holy Spirit,
you who descended on the apostles at Pentecost
and transformed them from cowards into martyrs.
Come upon me now, as I prepare this lesson.
Do not let my words be empty shells;
let every sentence I speak carry fire.
Grant me the gift of wisdom to say only what is needed,
the gift of understanding to explain with clarity,
the gift of counsel to respond with prudence,
and the gift of fortitude not to lose heart.
May these young people remember not me,
but the Holy Spirit acting through this lesson.
Like St. José Sánchez del Río, teach me to proclaim
with my life more than my voice: “Long live Christ the King!”
Amen.

Print Catechist Guide
